speaker: interviewer
text: So your wife overheard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she heard that, and he drowned, he drowned that girl. He drowned her. So later on, after we were free, we were in Jaworow. There was a lot of apartments, 1 the Jews were killed. A lot of Ukrainian people, Polish people took over Jewish homes. But there were some across the street, the magistrate, the Russians, where the Russian's police station was. There was an apartment. And so she asked him if she can get this apartment. And so my wife get this apartment. So when she was in here, so he build, she got, somebody came from Russia. They start coming back. Nobody was in the city. She was the first one. But then people start coming back from Russia. Once they took over, people from Jaworow. So her brother came and a lot of other friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, the Russians took me in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a rough time, being so long and leaving a wife and a step child, leaving alone after so many, so many harsh days. And they came and they took me. And they say, you going in the army. They cut my hair off.

speaker: interviewer
text: They forced you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I said, why? Just, I just came. I was, you know, after three, four weeks, you know. I just came. I got a foot, my foot was still not healed completely. So I say, here, listen, we came over. We lost a lot of blood. We saved you. And now you have to go fight and finish the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took a lot of other people. They took me and they took also a dentist was, he was my age, a dentist. And they cut off the hair. And they say, in a couple of days you'll go. So my wife was crying, and here where you leave me, with who you leave me, with banderas.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bunch of fighting goes on, it was going on a lot of fight with the Ukrainians banderas, with the Russian army. They were killing a lot of Russian officers. Sometime they kill a Russian officer, then they took the uniform. And they were wearing the uniform, that's everybody think that he's a Russian officer. But he was not a Russian. He was a bandera. They were killing Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then when my wife find out that he is here, this guy, so she went to the commissar, to the Russian, and she told him, here this man killed plenty of Jewish people. And I know which my girlfriend he killed. And he killed also a lot of Russian woman, what the officers had to run away in the beginning. They wouldn't go. They killed a lot of Russians. So they took him and they sent him to Siberia. She came to court in Lemberg so she had to tell her what's happened. Could you tell me now what happened to you, to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's happened to me, when they took me in the army, we were walking for five days, walking, till far away, till we came to Keltz. In Keltz, the Russian army stopped. They couldn't go. They went fast till Keltz. Ketlz they stopped because the German army was on top of the hills. And we were very low. And they were all, have all those armors, terrible shooting, and everything, so the Germans. Over there, they took us and we start building bunkers, you know, for the army. To go here, this is this battalion. This is this area, you know. So I was doing what, I was doing. I didn't build the bunkers, but I was making signs. Like in Poland, I used to make signs, you know, paint signs for the roads too, for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what was your main job with the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: My main job later on, later on, I was, they know I'm Jewish. And they asked me if I know German. I used to write and read very good German because my uncle, he teached us private, the German language. So I know, so I told him I know. So they send me away for six weeks. He sent me away to a special school. He send me away to know the ranks. I know how to speak. I know how to write, but I didn't know the ranks from the Russian officers and the high ranks and also the Secret Service, the Russians from the [INAUDIBLE] to all kind of secret intelligence. So I had to know the names and the ranks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The year and the time when this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the year this was in around November, December, around November, December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't ask you before, what date were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by the Russians we were liberated, then I think July, June or July. July we were liberated July.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it's December and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's around November. November we stayed October, November, and December, we stayed there. And then January 15, they make the offensive. They got prepared. They got prepared. They brought the Russian katyushas The Russian had on trucks special, special katyushas armors, that they can shoot 8, 16, or 32 at one time, push buttons. And it's very powerful. The Germans had [GERMAN] on six. So they overpowered the German army. Now, they also was connected because I worked in the [INAUDIBLE] from the officers, from the general, with all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which general was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: General was, he was not a general. Later, he became a general. He was a Jewish general, a Jewish major [? Leff. ?] His name was [? Leff. ?] He [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little, short little guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe how you used your German and how you helped the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how I worked, later translating, you know, translating later when they make the offensive. Now, the offensive was not just the Russians could do it. The Americans came with a lot airplanes, bombarded the whole area. Later after a half hour bombarding, bombarding, then our artillery, the Russian artillery and the katyushas start shooting. And that's the way we went. And I have to watch also for the mines. There was a lot of, the whole field was mines. Once we went to put in, to put in mines, to put it in from the Germans, the Germans spotted us and they start shooting. And I was also [INAUDIBLE] in the Russian army to make bandages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you can tell me a little bit of this later, but would you now tell me after you took those three months your work, what you did for the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: In there, it was not much to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, after you, how did they use your German?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the offensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after the offensive, when they make the 15th the offensive, later we keep going. And I have to tell them the roads or whatever there's mines, to read, to read the signs where there's mines. And there were some mines what exploded. Some Russian soldiers got killed or they went in the other way, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you liberate any cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, they liberate a lot of cities. We to liberate the whole Poland. We went through to whole Poland and Germany. So we went, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how you liberated a city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got sick. First of all, I got sick. Later on, I got sick. And I got sick, and I got a lot of fever. So we were going, we were going with the armory, with the gyps, you know, but later, captain say, you're going to get sicker and sicker. So you better, you stay in this city. I don't remember now the city. We stopped. Anyway, you stay in this city. And later on, somebody will direct you how to get to us. Now, when I stopped there for three days, and I felt already better after three days, I start going and asking where is this and this, was the 360 division from the Russian army. So and I start asking, nobody knows till I saw the Secret Service from the Russian, the Russian, the Russian army, Secret Service of the Russian army. They call them the guards. Anyway, so I stopped in their office. And I say, here, I want to catch up with my polk, with my regiment. I want to stop. I'm a translator for them. [INAUDIBLE] can you direct me, you should know, where my army is. He say, ja, yes, yes, OK. You stay for awhile with us. We have a lot to a lot of space here. You know, we'll give you a room and you stay with us. And I wait and I wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they don't want to send me. I said, why didn't you want to send me to my polk? He way, you know what, you know what, we know you went through it. You know, they asked me what and what about me. I'd say, went through. He say, you know what, we don't want you to get killed. You've suffered so much already in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the front, the first one what you go and you can be killed, you know, any minute. With us, is very seldom anybody of us get killed, because we don't go with the army fighting. We go just to make in governments every city. Poland, you don't have to do nothing in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you describe what happened to you, like what you did for them?

words.text: to you, like what you did for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they wanted them to be, so they want to be a translator. And they became a translator. And we were going from city to city setting up communist government, you know, in Germany. In Germany and went in Greenberg, [INAUDIBLE], all those cities. Then we came to Drazen. Drazen was a big city where the Americans bombarded very badly, like 250,000 people they say in both cities got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. We make our government. And we also used to, the soldiers, used to go catching Gestapo. We used to catch them, bring them to my office. I had a separate office where I interrogate them and find out who they are, what they did, whether they were officers, or where they were. So there was a lot of them. This we were going from city to city. Could you a describe how the interrogation took place and where did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interrogation was a little hard from the beginning. To hit, you have to hit them. You have to be very rough, because some Germans were very rough, very rough. Like the big generals and the majors, they don't want to say. They don't want to say what they did. The only one, the Gestapo we don't have to ask, because we checked them all. If they have numbers, they're the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you? Could you describe any general that you remember in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they say it's not our fault, and this is this, and this is that. And that, that's about all. They say it's not their fault. And they didn't do it. But how is [INAUDIBLE]. You know, like we, I got one interrogator, he was the head of Warsaw's, all the factories, all the factories, confiscating all the Jewish factories. And he was the head of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went right away with no brink or thing. Right away, they put a stamp into the papers and write, transferred them to Siberia. Most of them they transfer right away to Siberia. And also went to their to their homes. We went to their homes checking out whatever they have, their richness. They had a lot of jewelry, a lot of fur coats, what they grabbed in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the factory, what they confiscated. Jews have to get away. Like here, I had beautiful stuff and I had to give him. They say, we'll, if we'll find by any Jew, we'll find any force, right away you get killed. So a lot of people gave away stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you kill any SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I kill only in a time when we were going by Lodz. Before Lodz, [INAUDIBLE], you couldn't go through a place where they had two divisions the have in the woods. And they were shooting from all over. We couldn't go in. And later on, the Russians brought in those big katyushas, the Russian armors. And they start shooting, they start coming out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they start coming out, everybody, we have machine guns, we had. Because even we are not going to first, but we were all step up and watch with the soldiers. So when we saw them coming out, then they pick up their hands, or they pick up a white handkerchief, or whatever they pick up. So you go, just come on over. You take him. You arrest him. You take a couple of soldiers. They send them to a place. And later, they interrogate them. They let them go in Germany and some of them not, it depends who they are. Did you kill personally any SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then it happens, that's a lot of open up their hands and they came. They came closer, and they took out their guns. Their rifles, they throw away. But they had yet guns. And they start shooting. From that time, we never had it. We have to start, soon as they coming out, we have to shoot them. Just with machine gun, they falling like flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but interrogating, never killed nobody, just, you know, interrogation. The Russians are very interrogating. It was like squeezing, like squeezing, putting in between the fingers. And really hitting. They know where to hit, where to hit, and how to hit all kind, all kind. But I didn't know. They even teach me and say, how can you be? You are so, so soft with them. You tell us that they killed your mother and they killed your whole family, brothers, and sisters. Why you so nice? You know, what they did to us in Russia, we cannot forgive them. They burned whole villages, they burned when they came. They don't look for nothing, children. And that's the way they picked the Jewish children. My goodness, and they killed people without, without anything, so. Could When you're interrogating them, what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were rough, some were rough. It happens to my friend. He was a Polish, but you know, very good German also. So he had interrogating one, one. And he start hitting him, the German. He started hitting him. He was, he had a big run. But he was in his private life he was a butcher, very strong guy. So he start hitting him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once there was two Russian police, standing not inside, but standing outside by the door. They heard him hollering, so they opened up the door. And they shot him, right away they shot him to death, they shot him. This was my friend [INAUDIBLE]. Now, I'd like to, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened, that's all what I did, interrogate them and wrote the protocol from German to Russia. They did this, and this, and this, and this. But later, you know, confiscating, we were confiscating not only this, but gold, diamonds, jewelry, a whole group. Sometimes we sneaked in something in a pocket. It happens. But mostly, they picked up like at once about 20, 20 trains, 20, 20 trains to send to Russia. And they sent a lot of them, our polk, Secret Service Police, watching them through the road. So they also dig out later, a couple came back, officers. They told me that they got platinum and all kind of stuff, gold, you know. Not, but everything went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any, any one German officer that is important?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, oh, important, I didn't told you in the Ghetto, what I did in Poland. Wolff, Wolff, he was a harsh Nazi, killing Jews with the dogs. Just anybody got out from the Ghetto, what was not allowed. You have the Jewish, star, you know, and got out. As soon as he saw anybody outside, when he passed by with his dog, walking around almost every day. Not a whole day, but once in a while, walking around. When he saw you outside, right away he put the dog, dog on them and ripped them apart. Wolff was his name. He was the head of the Gestapo, the police in Jaworow. First he was in Krakow, but then he was in Jaworow.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now, any of the officers you interrogated, do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't remember no name. Oh God. I don't remember my old friends sometimes, but they were with me so many years, you forget things. Wolff, I remember. I remember my [INAUDIBLE] coming down

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you with the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Russian army, I was almost two years. Because they are supposed to let me off further, but we went all through Germany. We went to Drazen and Leipzig, because Leipzig was the American army. They made a pact, a pact with the Americans, because Leipzig, the old zone belongs to the Russian zone. So we were in Leipzig, when we came to Leipzig, it's a beautiful, the nicest city in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you when the war finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war finished? You know, I was in Germany. I was there in Leipzig, then we went to Weimar, Weimar where Hitler signed pacts. Wiemar pact. Weimar or [INAUDIBLE], up to the Berlin. We didn't go into Berlin. Other sections, other brigades went into other places. But we went only up to Berlin, to our [INAUDIBLE], Weimar or [INAUDIBLE]. And then, after that, they didn't let me off yet. We had to go back to Poland to [INAUDIBLE], and over there, finish up their work with the Polish government, with the Polish government. [INAUDIBLE] catching Nazis what they were want to stay in Poland, you know, they had family or whatever, interrogating them. In May 5th, 1945, 1946, they let me go. And I couldn't even tell my wife, write to her, because it was all secret, you know. She didn't know where I am, never never know, just a number, and that's it. But never know. So I didn't let her know that I come. Now, the 5th of May, they let me go with the train--

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Lemberg free to Lemberg, in 1946, 1946. So they let me go, and I came to [? Jaworow. ?] When I came to [? Jaworow-- ?] and not to [INAUDIBLE], to Lemberg before that, a couple of days before, my wife ran away from [? Jaworow. ?] She had a store, she was selling liquor, and baking bread, in delicatessen. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue to what happened to you, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this, this all come out, what's happened with my wife, how I find her. Because when the Russians, when you don't pay tax, if you make a lot of money, you have to pay tax. They found once in her drawer, in the money they found a lot of rubles. So she say, you have to pay $100,000 for the whole year. You have to pay $100,000. She didn't have the $100,000 tax to pay. So she ran away in middle of the night. She hired a truck and she went to Lemberg And in Lemberg she registered. All Polish Jews had the right, the Russians had to let them out to Poland, all Polish Jews. This was the pact, what they made with the Polish government. Now, when she came to Lemberg, she wrote me a letter, a letter that's and now come to Lemberg. Or she is going to be there or not, I should stop in this and this place. This was a Jewish officer, a big officer. He was in Lemberg and--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name [INAUDIBLE]. I still remember his name-- [INAUDIBLE], him and his wife and a daughter. So she left me a address. And I'll come to Lemberg, because she'll have to give away to Poland. I am not back. So here and this and this address, I should come. And he's going to direct me where she is going to be in Poland, because she's going to be corresponding to [INAUDIBLE], where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came before a couple of days, before she goes in the city to buy something, and there was a gypsy. And the gypsy said, come on, I'll tell you all your life story and everything. She say, all right. Maybe it's a good idea. How much you want? So she says $50. You want 50 rubles. That's a lot of money. So he said, you want to know the truth? That's it. So she gave her the $50.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing what she told her, you have a husband [INAUDIBLE]. They called me [INAUDIBLE], the Russians. You have a husband [INAUDIBLE]. He's coming the 5th of May. But she'd never know when I'm coming. He is coming. The Russian army let him go. And the 5th of May, he's going to arrive here. Now you're going to be very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're going to come to the United States. You're going to travel through an ocean faraway. You're going to have another child, you're going to have. And that's what he tell her. She gave him $50. Now, she came to the house and there was three more people, families, and she was in Lemberg, they supposed to go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: She told them the story. She said what, you're believing in a gypsy? How did, she say, how did he know Leon's name, [INAUDIBLE], and the date he is coming? She say, I'm going to go and check. And she went to a station. Lemberg had, a big city, it had three stations. She went to check with another station. And I was in another station. So when I came, and I went right with a, with a taxi-- horse and wagon those years, you know-- with a, with a taxi man. And he took me to that address. And I came there. They go, we're very happy. There was no-- oh yeah, they had a telephone. So they called my wife. But why don't you tell me how you met.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so he told, so-- no, no. They didn't telephone her, because they had a telephone. She didn't have a telephone where my wife was staying with the other families. So they send the girl, she was 15 years old, [INAUDIBLE], they sent the girl, it was about six, seven blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she ran over. And she opened up there. She say, Rosa, You know what's happened? She said what, there's a letter from my, from my husband? She said, no, how about your husband is here. Aw, this was already unbelievable. The [INAUDIBLE], like in Hebrew, and they all happiness. And we came together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the problem is one thing. She had already a list in three days to go to Poland. She took me and we went to that place where they give you the passports to go to Poland. So we went to that lady. It was the head of the place was a lady, very, very miserable person. And my wife said, here, my husband came. He was in the intelligence in the Russian army. He came back and I wanted to take him with me. She said you go to hell. You can go too. They were mad. The Russians were a lot of mad that Jews left and they went to Poland. She said, go to hell. Your husband has to go first to [? Jaworow, ?] take himself off of the military list. And then he'll come, he'll come. My wife start crying. It didn't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked out from there with a big cry. She was crying. And there just happened like an angel, one man came up, a tall, handsome Russian officer, not a Jewish, a Russian officer. And he says, [INAUDIBLE]. They call her Rosa. Ruthie is Rosa. What are you crying? What's happened? Who is this? He said, this is my husband who just came from the army. You know, he's a [INAUDIBLE] and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came out, where she chased me out. She doesn't want to write down my husband on my list, on my passport, which she could do. She was rough. So he say, don't worry. I'll take you tomorrow morning. I'll go with you, your husband, because if I'm not going, they wouldn't let you. There was a line for a mile, people going away, you know, Jewish people. So they say there's a line. So your husband has nothing to worry. He can come any time. But and we came upstairs, come to my office. Give me all the papers. I gave him all the papers. He write out, took me off, put me on, on her list. And he say, in three days I'm going to come with my-- he had a little station wagon-- I'll come with my station wagon. I'll take all you [INAUDIBLE] and I'll put you right in the wagon where you're supposed to go. This was then, we were really, my wife, open [INAUDIBLE]. And then you come there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now may, you can tell me some details later. Could you tell me what happened? When did you find out what happened to all your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the family members?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, not all your family members, but your mother, and your brothers, and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know there was nobody. There was nobody. There was no, nothing to ask. There was nobody. There was nobody, just who survived in Russia, like my wife's brother. He survived in Russia and he came back, so we were together. Otherwise, I know nobody is there. I know my brother, and I know my one brother, I know my sister, and mother and one brother, I know he was in Janov Ghetto got killed, in Janov Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, the older, David, my older brother was killed. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From all your family, from all the aunts, from all the uncles, how many people survived? What number?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my family? Well, from my family, I got a first cousin in Seattle, Washington. He was on Irish papers, when she act like Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one first cousin. And then there was another cousin survived in Russia, and came to Israel, and to the United States. A second cousin survived also in Germany, in a camp. She was like a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like not Jewish, also on paper. She survived. So about 1, 2, 3-- like 3, 3, 1, 2, 3, and I'm the fourth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Big family, hundreds, hundreds. My mother's family, very big. My father's family, also very big. Uncles, and aunts, and cousins, and second cousins, very rich family. I got a cousin, oh, yeah, one cousin also survived in Russia, in [INAUDIBLE] with her daughter she lives. The daughter and the mother died. The daughter lived in Montreal, what I got this and another survived in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your wife find out what happened to her first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, to her first husband, now what's happened, later on when my brother-in-law came, my brother-in-law came back from Russia. And he said to my wife, I heard that [INAUDIBLE] is alive. Somebody told him they saw him, you know [INAUDIBLE] is alive. He says, well, where is he? If he would be alive, he should have come.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Anyway in a couple of weeks later, [INAUDIBLE] comes in. And he say, this one here and there, you survived. He way, what's happened to you? [INAUDIBLE] and another guy told me, a guy what he died in Israel, very close friend. So he say, they said that you were, that you were dead, that you got killed in the first bombardment from the Russian, from the Germans got killed. It happened because we saw everybody, we never saw him no more. He was with us together. How did it happen? So nobody, nobody knows. He say it happened. I was injured, but not badly. And later on, I went to Russia. And he was also in Russia where. And [INAUDIBLE] my brother-in-law was in another place in [INAUDIBLE]. So what did your wife do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So later, he want he says it's all right. She say, here listen, you know, I got, I got another husband. I remarried. [INAUDIBLE]. He say, what? Nah, forget about it. No, you're my wife and this is my child. And this is it. She say, no, I live with Leon a couple of years. Never could happen that we could live together. Never in life. He was a little bit mad, here and there. But later, she say, instead of having arguments, she had some money. She had like the Russian gold, gold rubles. And she gave him gold rubles. And she said go better to Poland. You'll meet [INAUDIBLE]. Her brother was already there. And now, when we came, when we came there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we will take a pause--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to change tapes.
